chunk_summary: A clinical vignette of a 9-month-old with faltering growth due to diluted complementary feeds, focusing on dietary assessment, feeding skills evaluation, and management strategies to improve energy density, protein intake, and texture progression.
learning_objectives: Understand the impact of diluted complementary feeds on infant growth and nutrition.
Learn structured methods for dietary assessment and feeding observation in infants.
Develop management strategies to improve energy density, protein intake, and texture progression in complementary feeding.
Recognize when to escalate care to a dietitian or speech-language pathologist.
key_facts: The infant is 9 months old with 2 months of faltering weight gain.
Dietary recall shows low energy density and protein intake below age-appropriate needs.
Observation reveals runny food consistency and limited exposure to textured foods.
Management includes thickening foods, adding fat (e.g., oil or ghee), and introducing age-appropriate textures and finger foods.
Follow-up includes weight review and repeat dietary/feeding assessment in 2–4 weeks.
Escalation to a dietitian or SLP is recommended if weight velocity does not improve or safety concerns arise.
reference_quote: Guideline for complementary feeding of infants and young children 6–23 months of age (WHO): 'Complementary foods should be energy-dense, nutrient-rich, and age-appropriate in texture and consistency.'
